content.text: What the Resource Group Location Controls

A resource group's location (region) determines where Azure Resource Manager stores the group's metadata — the list of resources it contains, its tags, its locks, and its RBAC assignments. It does not constrain where the resources inside the group are physically deployed. You can have a resource group located in eastus that contains a VM in westeurope, a storage account in australiaeast, and a Cosmos DB account spanning six regions.

This is one of the most misunderstood aspects of Azure resource groups. The confusion arises because the Portal's 'Create' dialogs often pre-fill the resource location to match the resource group location, making it appear as if they are linked. They are not.

attrs.alt: Resource group location stores metadata only — resources can be in any region

content.text: Why Resource Group Location Matters for Control Plane

The resource group's location matters only for control plane resilience. If the Azure region hosting the resource group's metadata experiences an outage, ARM cannot process management operations — create, update, delete, or tag — for that group. The resources themselves continue running; only management-plane changes are blocked.
VM in eastus, RG in eastus, eastus outage
VM may be impacted AND ARM management blocked
VM in westus2, RG in eastus, eastus outage
VM continues running; ARM management blocked for the group
VM in westus2, RG in eastus, westus2 outage
VM may be impacted; ARM management for the group still works
Multi-region Cosmos DB, RG in eastus, eastus outage
Cosmos DB continues serving from other regions; ARM management blocked

For workloads that must remain manageable during a regional outage, consider placing the resource group in a different region from the primary workload — or use management groups and Azure Policy to enforce naming standards that make cross-region group location obvious.
How to Specify Resource Group Location

# Create a resource group in eastus
az group create --name myapp-prod --location eastus

# List available regions
az account list-locations --output table

# Show the location of an existing resource group
az group show --name myapp-prod --query location --output tsv

# You cannot change a resource group's location after creation
# The only option is to recreate the group in the desired region
Resources Can Span Multiple Regions Inside One Group

A single resource group can contain resources across all Azure regions. Global resources (like Azure Traffic Manager profiles, Azure Front Door, and Azure DNS zones) have no region — their location is 'global'. Regional resources each carry their own location property.

# Create resources in different regions, all in the same resource group
RG=myapp-prod

# VM in East US
az vm create \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --name vm-eus \
  --location eastus \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_B1s \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Storage account in West Europe
az storage account create \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --name myappstoragewe \
  --location westeurope \
  --sku Standard_LRS

# Traffic Manager profile (global — no region)
az network traffic-manager profile create \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --name myapp-tm \
  --routing-method Performance

# List all resources and their locations
az resource list \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Type:type, Location:location}' \
  --output table

# List all location names (for use in az commands)
az account list-locations --query '[].name' --output tsv | sort

# List locations with availability zone support
az account list-locations \
  --query '[?availabilityZoneMappings!=null].{Name:name, DisplayName:displayName}' \
  --output table

# Check if a specific resource type is available in a location
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='managedClusters'].locations[]" \
  --output tsv | grep -i 'east us'
Data Residency and the Resource Group Location

Data residency compliance (GDPR, HIPAA, local data laws) is determined by the location of each individual resource — not the resource group. A resource group located in westeurope does not guarantee that data stored by resources inside it stays in Europe. Each resource's own location governs where data physically resides.

# Verify the actual region of every resource in a group
az resource list \
  --resource-group myapp-prod \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Type:type, Region:location}' \
  --output table

# Find resources NOT in allowed regions (e.g. EU compliance check)
EU_REGIONS='northeurope westeurope uksouth ukwest francecentral francesouth germanywestcentral'
az resource list \
  --resource-group myapp-prod \
  --query "[?!(location=='northeurope' || location=='westeurope' || location=='uksouth')]"\
  --query "[].{Name:name, Type:type, Region:location}" \
  --output table
Enforcing Resource Location with Azure Policy

# Assign built-in policy: Allowed locations
# This restricts where resources CAN be created — not the RG location
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'eu-data-residency' \
  --scope /subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myapp-prod \
  --policy /providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4c \
  --params '{
    "listOfAllowedLocations": {
      "value": ["northeurope", "westeurope", "uksouth", "ukwest"]
    }
  }'

# After assignment, creating a resource in eastus in this group will be denied
az storage account create \
  --resource-group myapp-prod \
  --name mystorageeastus \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_LRS
# Error: disallowed by policy 'eu-data-residency'
The resource() Location in Bicep Templates

When deploying resources via Bicep or ARM templates, it is best practice to parameterize the resource location separately from the resource group location. Using resourceGroup().location as a default is convenient but can unexpectedly constrain resources to one region.
All workload resources in one region
Primary region; or a stable region unlikely to go down
Workload in region with no paired region (e.g. brazilsoutheast)
Any EU region — data residency is per-resource, not per-RG
Any region — global resources ignore RG location
Disaster recovery: RG must be manageable when primary fails
Different region from workload (e.g. workload in eastus, RG in westus2)

The resource group location is a low-stakes choice for most teams. Pick the region closest to your primary resources and move on. The only scenario where RG location matters significantly is when you need ARM management to remain available during a regional failure of your workload region — a rare requirement outside of strict SLA scenarios.
Azure Paired Regions and Resource Group Location

Each Azure region is paired with another region in the same geography for disaster recovery and data replication. When choosing a resource group location, placing it in one of a paired region set ensures ARM metadata stays geographically close to your workloads.

# Azure paired regions — each region is paired for geo-redundancy
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/locations?api-version=2022-12-01' \
  --query 'value[?metadata.pairedRegion!=null].[name, metadata.pairedRegion[0].name]' \
  --output table

# Common pairs:
# eastus       <-> westus
# eastus2      <-> centralus
# northeurope  <-> westeurope
# eastasia     <-> southeastasia
# uksouth      <-> ukwest
# australiaeast <-> australiasoutheast
For active-passive DR, create paired resource groups—one per region—using a subscription-scoped Bicep deployment:

During a primary region outage, fail over Traffic Manager (or Azure Front Door) to route traffic to the secondary. The secondary RG's ARM endpoint remains available because it lives in a separate region.

# Failover: redirect Traffic Manager to secondary endpoint
az network traffic-manager endpoint update \
  --profile-name myapp-tm \
  --resource-group myapp-shared-rg \
  --name primary-endpoint \
  --type azureEndpoints \
  --endpoint-status Disabled

az network traffic-manager endpoint update \
  --profile-name myapp-tm \
  --resource-group myapp-shared-rg \
  --name secondary-endpoint \
  --type azureEndpoints \
  --endpoint-status Enabled

# Verify active endpoint
az network traffic-manager endpoint list \
  --profile-name myapp-tm \
  --resource-group myapp-shared-rg \
  --query '[].{name:name, status:endpointStatus}' -o table
Azure Policy and Resource Group Location

Azure Policy operates at two distinct levels regarding location: the location of the resource group itself, and the locations of resources deployed into that group. These are controlled by separate policy definitions.
Restricting Resource Locations Within a Group
The built-in policy 'Allowed locations' (policy ID e56962a6...) restricts where resources inside a resource group can be deployed. The resource group's own location is unaffected by this policy.

# 1. Create an initiative that restricts allowed resource locations
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'allow-eu-locations' \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myapp-rg \
  --policy 'e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4f' \
  --params '{"listOfAllowedLocations":{"value":["northeurope","westeurope","uksouth"]}}'

# 2. Policy applies to RESOURCES inside the RG (not the RG itself)
# 3. Test: deploying a VM to eastus will be denied
az vm create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --name testvm \
  --location eastus \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --admin-username azureuser
# Error: RequestDisallowedByPolicy — location 'eastus' is not allowed

# 4. The RG location (northeurope) is NOT constrained by this policy
#    Only the resources deployed INTO the RG are constrained.

To control where resource groups themselves can be created, you need a separate custom policy that targets the Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourceGroups resource type and is assigned at subscription scope with mode 'All'.
Important: Assigning the 'Allowed locations' policy to a resource group does NOT constrain where that resource group's ARM metadata lives. It only constrains the resources you deploy into it.
Multi-Region AKS Under Resource Group Location Patterns

Kubernetes workloads often span multiple regions for high availability. The recommended pattern uses one resource group per AKS cluster (for lifecycle isolation) plus a shared resource group for global networking resources. Each resource group's location matches the cluster's region.

# Deploy AKS clusters to two regions under a single logical app group
# Best practice: separate RGs per cluster + shared networking RG

# Cluster 1 — primary (East US 2)
az group create --name myapp-aks-eastus2-rg --location eastus2
az aks create \
  --resource-group myapp-aks-eastus2-rg \
  --name myapp-aks-primary \
  --location eastus2 \
  --node-count 3 \
  --enable-cluster-autoscaler --min-count 2 --max-count 10

# Cluster 2 — secondary (West US 2, paired for geo-redundancy)
az group create --name myapp-aks-westus2-rg --location westus2
az aks create \
  --resource-group myapp-aks-westus2-rg \
  --name myapp-aks-secondary \
  --location westus2 \
  --node-count 2 \
  --enable-cluster-autoscaler --min-count 1 --max-count 5

# Shared networking resources (Front Door, DNS) — use the first cluster's region
az group create --name myapp-shared-rg --location eastus2
az afd profile create \
  --resource-group myapp-shared-rg \
  --profile-name myapp-frontdoor \
  --sku Standard_AzureFrontDoor
After provisioning, merge both cluster configs and deploy manifests to each:

# Merge both cluster credentials
az aks get-credentials --resource-group myapp-aks-eastus2-rg --name myapp-aks-primary
az aks get-credentials --resource-group myapp-aks-westus2-rg --name myapp-aks-secondary

# Switch between contexts
kubectl config use-context myapp-aks-primary
kubectl apply -f manifests/

kubectl config use-context myapp-aks-secondary
kubectl apply -f manifests/

# Use Flux or ArgoCD to sync manifests to both clusters automatically
flux bootstrap github \
  --owner=myorg \
  --repository=myapp-gitops \
  --branch=main \
  --path=clusters/eastus2
Front Door, DNS, ACR, Key Vault
Auditing and Remediating Resource Group Locations
In large organizations, resource groups can accumulate in unapproved regions over time—especially before location policies were applied. Use the Azure CLI or Python SDK to audit and remediate.

Resource group locations are immutable—you cannot change them after creation. The only path to a different location is to create a new resource group and move resources into it. Not all resource types support Move operations; validate first.

# You CANNOT change a resource group's location after creation.
# Workaround: create new RG in target region, move resources, delete old RG.

# Step 1: Create new RG in desired location
az group create --name myapp-rg-new --location westeurope

# Step 2: Identify movable resources (not all resources support Move)
az resource list --resource-group myapp-rg-old \
  --query '[].id' --output tsv > resources.txt

# Step 3: Validate the move before executing
az resource invoke-action \
  --action validateMoveResources \
  --ids /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myapp-rg-old \
  --request-body '{"resources":["<resourceId1>","<resourceId2>"],"targetResourceGroup":"/subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myapp-rg-new"}'

# Step 4: Move resources
az resource move \
  --destination-group myapp-rg-new \
  --ids $(cat resources.txt | tr '\n' ' ')

# Step 5: Delete old RG (only after verifying all resources moved successfully)
az group delete --name myapp-rg-old --yes --no-wait
Resources that DO support Move: VMs (stopped), Storage Accounts, App Service Plans, SQL Servers, Key Vaults, AKS clusters
Resources that DO NOT support Move: Azure Active Directory, Azure Backup Vaults with items, Application Gateways (some SKUs), Azure Bastion
Some resources require the subscription to be the same; cross-subscription Move is more restricted
After a Move, resource IDs change — update any ARM templates, Terraform state, or scripts that reference the old IDs

A resource group's location stores ARM metadata for that group — it does not determine where resources inside the group run or where their data lives. Resources in one group can span all Azure regions. Choose the RG location based on control plane resilience needs: place it in the same region as your primary workload for simplicity, or in a different region if you need ARM management to survive a regional failure. Data residency compliance is enforced per-resource (via resource location and Azure Policy allowed-locations assignments), not by the resource group's location.
